items.q: Why did Caribbean migrants come to Britain in 1948 on the Windrush?
items.correct: Britain faced post-war labour shortages; the 1948 British Nationality Act gave Commonwealth citizens UK residency rights and Caribbean migrants answered the call to rebuild.
items.misconception: Windrush migrants were illegal immigrants from outside the empire.
items.random_wrong: Windrush migrants came as refugees from Caribbean famines.
items.why: They arrived as British subjects with full legal rights — the irony of the modern 'Windrush scandal'. Students miss the citizenship framework that made the migration unproblematic in law.

items.q: Why did Britain restrict Commonwealth immigration from 1962?
items.correct: Rising public hostility, political pressure, and economic concerns led to the 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act introducing voucher controls, followed by tighter restrictions in 1968 and 1971.
items.misconception: Britain restricted Commonwealth immigration because there was no work available.
items.random_wrong: Britain restricted immigration because the Commonwealth ceased to exist in 1962.
items.why: There was labour demand but political backlash drove restriction. Students assume economics drives policy; in fact race politics and public opinion were decisive.

items.q: Why did Britain win the Battle of Britain?
items.correct: Radar, the Dowding command system, home advantage in pilot recovery, German tactical errors (switching to bombing cities), and adequate fighter production.
items.misconception: The RAF won because Spitfires were better than any German aircraft.
items.random_wrong: Hitler called off the attack to invade the USSR immediately.
items.why: The Bf 109 was comparable to the Spitfire. Victory came from integrated air defence and German strategic mistakes, not aircraft superiority — a key point in modern historiography.
The answer examiners want is: Falklands. “Gulf War” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What did the Community Charge of 1989-90 become known as?
items.why: It's Poll Tax. “VAT” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Tony Blair lead Labour to victory?
items.why: The real figure is 1997. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
